$${{{V_1} - 0} \over {{R_1}}} + {C_1} \times {d \over {dt}}({V_{out}} - 0) = 0$$

$${{d{V_{out}}} \over {dt}} = - {{{V_i}} \over {{R_1}{C_1}}}$$

VA = 0

$${V_{out}} = - {1 \over {{R_1}{C_1}}}\int {{V_i}dt} $$

Vout = $$-$$k $$\times$$ t

The output will be constant i.e. $$-$$VEE.

content: In the circuit, shown below, if the values of $R$ and $C$ are very large, the form of the output voltage for a very high frequency square wave input, is best represented by
explanation:
Given : The values of $R$ and $C$ are very large.

The given circuit is a high-pass $R C$ circuit.

For high frequency square wave, the circuit acts as differentiator.

A high-pass $R C$ circuit differentiates the input signal if the time constant ( $\tau=R C$ ) is large compared to the signal period.

The output is approximately the derivative of the input voltage for highfrequency signals.

From option (A) : Spikes at transitions

This would be correct for a perfect differentiator (when $R C$ is very small).

In an ideal differentiator the output would be sharp spikes at the transitions of the square wave.

From option (B) : Square wave output

If the circuit were simply passing the input without modification, the output would be the same as the input (square wave).

This option is incorrect because a high pass filter does not pass the input directly.

From option (C) : Triangular waveform

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content: The op-amps in the following circuit are ideal. The voltage gain of the circuit is ___________. (Round off to the nearest integer)
explanation:
$$Assume\,\, V_{\text {in }}=V_2-V_1 $$

Op-amp $A_1$ :

$$ V_X=\left(1+\frac{10 k}{10 k}\right) V_1=2 V_1 $$

Op-amp $A_2$ :

$$ \mathrm{KCL} \text { at } A_2 \quad \frac{V_X-V_2}{10 k}=\frac{V_2-V_0}{10 k} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} V_X & =2 V_2-V_0 \\ V_0 & =2 V_2-V_X=2 V_2-2 V_1=2\left(V_2-V_1\right) \\ \frac{V_0}{V_2-V_1} & =2 \end{aligned} $$

options.content: The maximum and minimum values of the output voltage $$V_0$$ are +15 V and $$-$$10 V, respectively.
The maximum and minimum values of the output voltage $$V_0$$ are +5 V and $$-$$15 V, respectively.
The maximum and minimum values of the output voltage $$V_0$$ are +10 V and $$-$$5 V, respectively.
The maximum and minimum values of the output voltage $$V_0$$ are +5 V and $$-$$10 V, respectively.

explanation:
Given circuit is,

For positive half cycle the diodes $D_1$ and $D_z$ are forward bias and $D_2$ is reverse biased and the circuit is shown below,

When $V_{\text {in }}=10 \mathrm{~V}$,

$$ V_0=\frac{-R}{R} \times 10=-10 \mathrm{~V} $$

$\therefore V_0$ will be -10 V .

For negative half cycle, diode $D_2$ is forward bias and $D_1, D_z$ is reverse bias. Zener diode is in breakdown region and the circuit is shown below,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore V_0=5 \mathrm{~V} \\\\ & \Rightarrow V_{0_{\max }}=5 \mathrm{~V} \text { and } V_{0_{\min }}=-10 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (D).

content: The output impedance of a non-ideal operational amplifier is denoted by Zout. The variation in the magnitude of Zout with increasing frequency, f, in the circuit shown below, is best represented by
explanation:
Analyais:

It is a voltage series feedback,

Voltage is a shunt connection,

Zout feedback, $${Z_{of}} = {{{Z_o}} \over {1 + A}} = {{{Z_o}} \over {1 + A}}$$ [Buffer, $$\beta$$ = 1]

A = Open loop gain

A = constant (low frequency)

$${Z_{of}} = {{{Z_o}} \over {1 + A}}$$ [Constant]

A = Decreasing

$$f \to {Z_{of}} \uparrow = {{{Z_o}} \over {1 + A \downarrow }}$$

$$A = 0$$

$${Z_{of}} = {{{Z_o}} \over {1 + 0}} = {Z_o}$$

content: The current gain (Iout/Iin) in the circuit with an ideal current amplifier given below is
explanation:
$${V_x} = {1 \over {{C_f}}}\int {{I_{in}} \times dt = {{{I_{in}}} \over {{C_f}}} \times t} $$

$${I_{out}} = {C_c} \times {{d{V_x}} \over {dt}} = {C_c} \times {d \over {dt}}\left( {{{{I_{in}}} \over {{C_f}}} \times t} \right)$$

$$ = {C_c} \times {{{I_{in}}} \over {{C_f}}}$$

$${{{I_{out}}} \over {{I_{in}}}} = {{{C_c}} \over {{C_f}}}$$
